title: South Korea
dek: Eight days based in Seoul — palaces, markets, gaming districts and street food — with a weekend in Daejeon for MSI 2026. Everything below is checked; tick items off as you go and your progress saves.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and license.
intro: Seoul base, with a two-night trip to Daejeon for the MSI 2026 weekend (Jul 11–12).

sections.title: When you land
sections.body: You land at Terminal 1, Incheon Airport (~05:30–06:30 KST on Thursday Jul 9). Air Premia YP132 arrives at T1 — the AREX station is inside the terminal, no shuttle needed. Budget 30–60 min through immigration at this hour. In order:
checklist: Scan your e-Arrival Card QR at the automated immigration gate (saved offline on your phone — done Jul 7)
Pick up a T-money card at any convenience store or ticket machine in arrivals; load ≈₩20,000

Take the AREX All-Stop from T1 directly to Hongik University Station (≈49 min, ≈₩4,050 — trains every 5–15 min from 05:14 onward). Do not board the Express — it skips Hongik University and terminates at Seoul Station only
Exit at Hongik University Station — Melody House (282-6 Seogyo-dong, Mapo-gu) is a 10–15 min walk from Exit 3 or 9, or a 5-min taxi (≈₩5,000–6,000). With luggage at 06:00, take the taxi
Text TRAVEL to 4004 to switch on Verizon TravelPass
Open KakaoMap or Naver Map — Google Maps navigation is very limited in Korea

sections.title: Local essentials
sections.body: Emergencies: 112 (police), 119 (fire & ambulance). Tap water is officially drinkable, though many locals prefer filtered or bottled. Tipping isn't expected. For maps use KakaoMap or Naver Map; for taxis, Kakao T; for translation, Papago. A little Korean goes far: annyeonghaseyo (hello), gamsahamnida (thank you).

sections.title: Entry & documents
sections.body: ⚠️ The entry rules below assume a US passport. On another passport, check your own requirements — they differ.
checklist: No visa for tourism up to 90 days
K-ETA is waived for US citizens through Dec 31, 2026 — you don't need it
Without a K-ETA you must complete the free e-Arrival Card within 3 days before arrival at the official site (e-arrivalcard.go.kr) — ignore lookalike sites that charge a fee
Optional: you may still apply for a K-ETA (₩10,000, ~$8, valid 3 years) to skip the arrival card
Passport valid for the length of your stay
Carry your MSI ticket confirmations and a power adapter (Korea uses Type C/F, 220V)

sections.title: Pre-flight countdown — specific actions by date
sections.body:
Time-sensitive. Do these in order — the e-Arrival Card window opens exactly 72 h before landing; do not complete it earlier or it expires.

Now — before Jul 7
Book the KTX Seoul→Daejeon (Jul 11, morning departure ≈08:30–09:00 — 2 tickets only; one person stays in Seoul for GO Fest) and Daejeon→Seoul return (Jul 13, morning — you stay a second night so there's no Sunday-evening rush) at korail.com or the KorailTalk app — Finals weekend trains fill fast. Your Daejeon I-Hotel (27-5 Expo-ro 123beon-gil, Yuseong-gu) is booked for both nights (Jul 11 & 12) — it sits ~130 m / a 2-minute walk from DCC II, so you drop your bags and stroll to both matches. Book the Changdeokgung Secret Garden (Huwon) 10:15 English tour for Tue Jul 14 — official info via cdg.go.kr (50 online slots per session; sells out). Install the NOL World app and confirm MSI tickets open (mobile-only — no screenshots, no prints).

Jul 5 (Sun) — 3 days before
Start the jet lag clock: go to bed 1 hour earlier than normal tonight, wake 1 hour earlier. Get 15–20 min of bright outdoor light within 30 min of waking. Dim screens after 20:00. Take 0.5–1 mg melatonin ~5 h before your new (earlier) bedtime. Stock Pokémon GO: Max Revives, Ultra Balls, Golden Razz, Silver Pinaps, Charged TMs. Build and save raid teams for Mega Mewtwo X (Flying/Fairy) and Y (Ghost/Dark).

Jul 6 (Mon) — 2 days before
Jet lag: go to bed 2 hours earlier. Same morning light + evening dim + melatonin protocol. Clear Pokémon storage to 400+ (transfer/release before the trip).

Jul 6–7 — within 3 days of landing
Complete the e-Arrival Card at e-arrivalcard.go.kr (free, 5 min). The site accepts submissions from ~3 days (72 h) before arrival — for your ~05:30 KST Jul 9 landing that window opens around Jul 6; don't try earlier or it won't be accepted, and don't leave it to the airport. Save the QR code to your camera roll offline. Jet lag: bedtime 3 hours earlier if possible. No caffeine after 12:00. Charge all battery packs and GO Plus/Gotcha. Pack: flip-flops (for jimjilbang), compact umbrella, quick-dry layers, insoles.

Jul 8 (Wed) — departure night
Depart Newark Liberty Airport (EWR) — not JFK. Air Premia flight YP132. K-ETA is waived for US citizens through Dec 31 2026 (no action needed). Check your booking confirmation for the EWR terminal. Check-in opens ~6 h before departure (≈19:30 Jul 7 EDT). Arrive at EWR by 23:00 at the latest for a smooth international process.

sections.title: Jet lag protocol — EWR to Seoul (13-hour eastward shift)
sections.body:
A 13-hour eastward shift is near worst-case for jet lag. Natural adaptation: ~1 hour per day = 13 days to fully adjust. With this protocol: 4–5 days. Three tools: timed light exposure, melatonin, and sleep scheduling. Sources: Frontiers in Physiology (2019), PMC1249488/PMC1262683, CDC Yellow Book Travel Health, Huberman Lab.

Pre-flight (Jul 5–7): Shift bedtime 1 hour earlier each night (see Pre-flight countdown above). Each morning get bright outdoor light within 15–30 min of waking — this is the strongest phase-advance signal available. Dim screens after 20:00 each evening (blocks the delay signals fighting your advance). Take 0.5–1 mg melatonin approximately 5 hours before your shifted bedtime — timing is critical; at this point melatonin produces maximum clock advance. Taking it too late works against you.

On the flight (01:30+ EDT Jul 8): Sleep is the goal. Eye mask and earplugs on immediately. Keep window shade closed — the polar route crosses near-continuous daylight at altitude. No alcohol: it suppresses REM and fragments sleep. No caffeine from boarding onward. Drink ~250 ml water per hour (cabin humidity is under 20%). Take 0.5–1 mg melatonin shortly after takeoff (~02:00 EDT / ~15:00 KST). Your body still reads this as ~2 a.m., so the priority on this leg is simply to sleep the overnight flight — melatonin helps you do that. (The real Seoul re-anchoring happens with morning light after you land.) When breakfast service begins near landing, eat it: meal timing helps shift your metabolic clock to Seoul rhythms.

Day 1 in Seoul (Jul 9 landing): Your internal clock says ~17:00 EDT (early evening New York). Do not nap on arrival — a full sleep cycle now locks you to NY time for days. Get outside within 1 hour of reaching your room: even 15–20 min of outdoor morning light (07:00–10:00 KST) is your strongest circadian anchor. Caffeine: use it in the morning and early afternoon only — hard cutoff at 14:00 KST. Stay active; don't sit still. Avoid bright outdoor light 17:00–19:00 KST for the first 2 days — on your NY body clock this is just-after-sunrise, and light here delays rather than advances your clock. Jimjilbang visit 17:00–21:00 (see Day 1 itinerary): the heat-then-cooling cycle triggers the temperature drop that signals sleep onset — one of the best jet-lag recovery tools available. Target bedtime: 22:00–22:30 KST. Take 0.5–1 mg melatonin at 21:00 KST. Repeat nightly for 4–5 nights.
checklist: Jul 5: Bedtime 1h earlier, morning outdoor light within 30 min of waking, dim evenings, melatonin 5h before new bedtime
Jul 6: Bedtime 2h earlier — same protocol
Jul 7: Bedtime 3h earlier; no caffeine after 12:00
On flight: eye mask on, no alcohol, water every hour, melatonin at ~02:00 EDT, eat Seoul-time breakfast near landing
Jul 9 AM: Outside for light by 09:00 KST — no nap over 30 min, caffeine cut off at 14:00 KST
Jul 9 PM: Jimjilbang 17:00–21:00 KST, melatonin 0.5–1 mg at 21:00, bed by 22:30
Jul 10–13: Morning outdoor light daily; avoid light 17:00–19:00 KST first 2 days; melatonin 21:00 nightly

sections.title: Phone & data
sections.body:
Verizon TravelPass — text TRAVEL to 4004 to activate ($12/line/day). Easy and reliable. A local eSIM is roughly half the price if your phone is unlocked:

  • Korean eSIMs from KT, SKT, or LG U+ run about ₩26,000–35,000 (~$17–23) for 7–10 days of unlimited LTE data. Speed throttles to 5 Mbps after 3 GB/day but stays usable for maps and translation. Buy in advance at KT Roaming, Trazy, or KKday — or at a telecom counter in ICN T1 arrivals (open from ≈07:00 KST on arrival morning). Confirm your phone is carrier-unlocked before you leave home.
  • Resellers ByteSIM and TooSIM often have the same networks at slightly lower prices with English checkout.

Apps that actually matter: KakaoMap or Naver Map for directions (Google Maps routing is severely limited in Korea), Kakao T for taxis, Papago for translation — download the Korean offline language pack before you leave (Papago → Languages → Korean → Download), Korail or KorailTalk for KTX tickets.

sections.title: Weather & comfort
sections.body:
Early-to-mid July is Korea's monsoon season (jangma) — hot (30–35 °C / 86–95 °F), very humid, and rainy. Gear up accordingly:

  • Pack a compact umbrella — heavy rain can hit without warning and briefly flood low streets or cause metro delays. Check the morning forecast.
  • Moisture-wicking, quick-dry clothes — linen or synthetic shirts, not cotton. Cotton stays wet all day in high humidity. One change of shirt per day minimum.
  • Hydrate constantly — convenience stores have cold water and sports drinks every 200m; use them. Heat exhaustion is a real risk by day 3 if you're walking all day.
  • Build in AC breaks every 2 hours: a café, a mall (Lotte Young Plaza, Shinsegae, Coex), or a museum. These are free and essential, not optional.
  • Weather app: use WeatherKorea or the KMA official app (kma.go.kr) — Korean meteorological forecasts are more accurate than international apps for localised monsoon patterns.

sections.title: Holidays & your dates
sections.body:
Timing is in your favour: no public holidays fall during 8–15 July 2026. Constitution Day (17 July) isn't a day off and lands just after you leave, and the next real holiday is Liberation Day on 15 August — so nothing shuts down while you're there.

What you do need to plan around is the weather. Mid-July is the peak of Korea's monsoon, 'jangma' — the wettest, most humid stretch of the year, with roughly 300–400 mm of rain across about 16 days, real heat, and humidity near 80%. The rain often arrives in heavy bursts that can briefly flood streets or slow transit, with a small typhoon chance later in the month. Pack a compact umbrella and quick-dry layers, keep indoor options in your back pocket, and check the forecast each morning. Your MSI weekend in Daejeon (11–12 July) is indoors, so rain won't touch it. (See Weather & comfort for what to wear.)

sections.title: Rainy day fallback — when the monsoon hits
sections.body:
Expect at least 1–2 full-rain days in July. The rain can be heavy but rarely lasts the whole day. Have a plan ready:

  • Stay underground: Seoul's subway-connected underground malls are enormous. The Hongdae Shopping Street (under Hongik University Station) and Gangnam's COEX Underground Mall are both walkable and free to enter — good for an entire afternoon.
  • COEX Aquarium (Samseong Station, Line 2): 45,000+ sea creatures, 2–3 hours easy. ≈₩33,000–35,000 pp — ⚠ verify price and book online to skip the queue. Open daily.
  • Seoul Museum of Art (SeMA) (City Hall, Line 1/2): free admission for main exhibitions, strong contemporary Korean art, fully dry. Takes 1.5–2 hours.
  • PC Bang: Full-spec gaming PCs, ≈₩1,500–2,500/hr, unlimited snacks at the counter. There's one within 5 minutes of any Hongdae station exit. Unironically one of the best rainy-day options for this group.
  • Cinema: CGV or Megabox near Hapjeong Station (one stop from Hongdae). Korean cinemas are excellent — huge screens, reclining seats, ≈₩12,000–15,000 pp. Both apps have English filter for subtitled showings.
  • Rainy day = great raiding: Fewer players competing for gyms. Head to COEX, Times Square (Yeongdeungpo, Line 1/5), or Lotte World Tower (Jamsil, Line 2/8) — all have indoor gyms and PokéStops. Light rain also boosts Water-type Incense spawns.

sections.title: Money & currency
sections.body:
Korea uses the won (₩). The won is weak right now, which helps your spending power — roughly (approximate, check before you go): ₩1,000 ≈ $0.67 and ₩10,000 ≈ $6.70, at about ₩1,500 to the dollar.

Cards and contactless work almost everywhere. For cash, the most reliable option is an ATM inside a GS25 or CU convenience store — both accept most foreign cards and charge modest fees. Global ATM Alliance banks (KEB Hana is the Korean partner) have lower or zero foreign withdrawal fees if your home bank is in the network — check before you leave. Avoid airport exchange booths; the rates are poor. Keep ≈₩30,000–50,000 cash for markets and small eateries. A T-money card covers all subway and buses. Tipping isn't expected anywhere.

sections.title: Health & pharmacy
sections.body:
Emergencies: dial 119 for ambulance or fire (free) and 112 for police. On a 119 call, say 'English, please' to reach an interpreter — your location is auto-detected. Save 1330 first: the 24/7 Korea Travel Hotline, English-speaking, can interpret, give directions, and patch you through to 119 or 112. 1339 is a 24-hour medical line that locates the nearest ER.

US Embassy Seoul — for lost passports, serious medical emergencies, or legal trouble: 188 Sejong-daero, Jongno-gu (between Gyeongbokgung and City Hall). Emergency line: +82-2-397-4114 (24/7). Website: kr.usembassy.gov. If you lose your passport in Daejeon, go to the Embassy in Seoul — there's no consulate in Daejeon.

Pharmacies (약국, yakguk) show a '약' sign. Many close Sundays but major-station branches stay open; some run 24 hours. Convenience stores cover paracetamol and antacids after hours. Korea bills upfront — no insurance card means 100% out of pocket.
checklist: Save 119 (ambulance/fire), 112 (police), 1330 (24/7 English travel hotline), 1339 (24-hr medical ER finder)
US Embassy Seoul — 188 Sejong-daero, Jongno-gu · +82-2-397-4114 (24/7) — for lost passports or serious emergencies
Buy travel insurance — without it you pay 100% upfront
Pack medicines in original packaging, plus a note of their generic names
Learn the sign: 약 = pharmacy; convenience stores cover basics after hours

sections.title: Etiquette & language
sections.body:
Korean is the language; signs, the subway, and major sights are bilingual. For translation, use Papago — it's far more accurate than Google Translate for menus and signs. Google Maps barely works for directions in Korea — use KakaoMap or Naver Map.

  • Bowing: a slight nod works as hello, thank you, or goodbye — don't overthink it.
  • Two-handed exchange: give and receive cards, cash, or items with both hands (or right hand with the left touching your forearm) — it signals respect.
  • No tipping, anywhere. Genuinely none — attempting it can create awkwardness. The price on the menu is what you pay.
  • Chopstick etiquette: don't stand them upright in rice (funeral association); rest them on the holder when you're not using them.
  • Pouring drinks: pour for others at the table; let them pour for you. Don't self-pour at a shared meal.
  • Shoes off: watch for a raised floor at traditional floor-seating restaurants — it's obvious when you see it.
  • Volume: keep it quiet indoors, especially on transit.
  • Subway rules: no eating or drinking on the metro — even water bottles, technically. Pink seats at the end of every car are priority seats for elderly, disabled, and pregnant passengers — don't sit in them even if the car is empty. You'll notice even young Koreans avoid them. Phones on silent; keep conversations brief and quiet.
  • Papago offline: before you leave home, open Papago → Languages → Korean → download the offline pack. Camera translation still needs a data signal, but text input works offline — useful in rural Daejeon areas.

Key phrases (Hangul + romanisation):

  • 안녕하세요 — annyeonghaseyo — hello
  • 감사합니다 — gamsahamnida — thank you (formal)
  • 얼마예요? — eolmayeyo? — how much?
  • 이거 주세요 — igeo juseyo — this one, please
  • 화장실 어디예요? — hwajangsil eodiyeyo? — where is the bathroom?
  • 괜찮아요 — gwaenchanayo — it's okay / no problem

sections.title: Navigating Seoul as first-time foreigners
sections.body:
Seoul is genuinely safe and hospitable for international tourists — the vast majority of your interactions will be frictionless. A few real things worth knowing going in:

  • Language gap vs. hostility. If someone seems reluctant or brushes you off, it's almost always communication anxiety, not unwillingness to serve you. Open Papago's camera mode, point it at the menu or sign, and the translation overlays live. Showing your phone screen with a typed phrase resolves most moments instantly. Most staff near tourist areas have functional English anyway.
  • Tattoos. Korean culture has historically associated visible tattoos with organized crime; this is changing among younger Koreans, but traditional jimjilbangs, some public pools, and older gyms still enforce no-tattoo policies. Check SPAREX Dongdaemun's current policy on their Klook listing before Day 1 if this applies to any of you. Casual restaurants, convenience stores, and the venues in this guide are unbothered.
  • Nightlife is not uniform. Hongdae and Sinchon clubs are genuinely inclusive — diverse crowds, entry based on ID. Some high-end Gangnam clubs have well-documented discriminatory door policies, including specific exclusion of Black individuals; this has been reported in both Korean and international media for years. The practical answer: Hongdae is your nightlife district for this trip, and it's excellent. Save Gangnam club nights for another trip with a Korean contact who can navigate the door. Noraebangs (private karaoke rooms) and PC bangs are universally welcoming regardless of neighbourhood.
  • Restaurants. Almost no restaurant in Seoul refuses service to foreigners. Even very local spots will manage — worst case, you get a picture menu and point. Ordering mechanics are in the Food section below.
  • If refused anywhere: don't escalate. Seoul is so dense with alternatives that something equivalent is within a 2-minute walk. Move on.

sections.title: What to wear — July in Korea
sections.body:
July is 30–35 °C and brutally humid. What you pack will make or break day 3 when the heat exhaustion hits.

  • Shirts: linen or moisture-wicking synthetic only. Cotton saturates and stays wet all day in 80% humidity — you'll feel it by noon. Pack one quick-dry shirt per day minimum; they rinse and dry overnight in the hotel.
  • Bottoms: lightweight shorts or thin chinos — both are fine in Seoul. Shorts are totally normal in the city; Korean dress culture is casual outside of formal workplaces. Avoid jeans in July: they trap heat and dry slowly.
  • Shoes: comfortable walking shoes with broken-in soles. You'll walk 10–15 km/day on mixed surfaces (stone palace courtyards, uneven sidewalks, tiled subway stations). Sandals are fine for Hongdae evenings but not a full sightseeing day. If any of you haven't broken in your shoes yet — do it this week.
  • Compact folding umbrella — non-negotiable. Heavy rain hits without warning during jangma. A good compact umbrella folds to 25 cm and costs under $15 at home; convenience stores sell them but they're flimsy.
  • Indoor layer (thin jacket or cardigan). Korean malls, restaurants, subway trains, and arenas run AC at 16–18 °C. The temperature swing between outside (34 °C) and inside (18 °C) is the most common reason people get sick mid-trip. Keep a light layer in your bag at all times — critical for DCC II during MSI.
  • MSI / arena: team jerseys and esports gear are welcome; no dress code at DCC II beyond the no-huge-bag rule. Bring that AC layer.
  • Jimjilbang (Day 1, SPAREX): the facility provides shorts and a T-shirt — you need nothing special except flip-flops for walking between the pools. Leave your shoes at the locker.
  • Daejeon day: same as Seoul. You'll be outdoors at EXPO Science Park for 6+ hours before the match — quick-dry fabric matters most on Saturday.

sections.title: Language guide — speaking Korean
sections.body:
English is widely spoken in central Seoul and tourist areas; much less common in Daejeon outside DCC II. You don't need to be fluent — three tools handle almost everything: Papago (camera translation; download Korean offline pack before you leave), picture menus (point and hold up fingers), and a handful of phrases below. Koreans respond warmly to any attempt, however imperfect.

Pronunciation note: stress is fairly even across syllables. Vowels are consistent: a = 'ah', eo = 'uh', eu = lips-back 'uh', i = 'ee', u = 'oo'.

Greetings & basics

  • 안녕하세요 — annyeonghaseyo — hello (universal; a slight nod with this is perfect)
  • 감사합니다 — gamsahamnida — thank you (formal)
  • 괜찮아요 — gwaenchanayo — it's okay / I'm fine / no problem
  • 네 — ne — yes  ·  아니요 — aniyo — no
  • 죄송합니다 — joesonghamnida — I'm sorry / excuse me (bumping into someone)
  • 잠깐만요 — jamkkanmanyo — just a moment / one second

Restaurants & cafes

  • 여기요! — yeogi-yo! — over here! (the universal way to call a server — normal and expected)
  • 이거 주세요 — igeo juseyo — this one, please (point at the menu)
  • 두 개 주세요 — du gae juseyo — two of these, please (hold up fingers to show number)
  • 맵지 않게 해주세요 — maepji ank-e haejuseyo — not spicy, please (show this on Papago if unsure of pronunciation)
  • 물 주세요 — mul juseyo — water, please
  • 계산해 주세요 — gyesan haejuseyo — the bill, please (or just walk to the counter)
  • 얼마예요? — eolmayeyo? — how much is this?
  • 카드 돼요? — kadeu dwaeyo? — do you accept card?

Shopping

  • 봐도 돼요? — bwado dwaeyo? — may I look / try this?
  • 이거 사이즈 있어요? — igeo saijeu isseoyo? — do you have this in my size?
  • 더 싸게 해주세요 — deo ssage haejuseyo — can you give a discount? (markets only)
  • 영수증 주세요 — yeongsujeung juseyo — receipt, please (keep for VAT refund)

Transit & directions

  • 화장실 어디예요? — hwajangsil eodiyeyo? — where is the bathroom?
  • 여기로 가 주세요 — yeogiro ga juseyo — please take me here (show address on phone to driver)
  • 미터기로 가주세요 — miteo-giro gajuseyo — please use the meter (taxis)
  • 세워 주세요 — seweo juseyo — please stop here
  • 영어 하세요? — yeong-eo haseyo? — do you speak English?

Emergencies

  • 도와주세요! — dowajuseyo! — help me!
  • 경찰 불러주세요 — gyeongchal bulleojuseyo — please call the police (112)
  • 구급차 불러주세요 — gugeupcha bulleojuseyo — please call an ambulance (119)
  • 아파요 — apayo — I'm sick / it hurts
  • 병원 어디예요? — byeongwon eodiyeyo? — where is the hospital?

sections.title: What staff will say to you
sections.body:
When you walk into a restaurant, shop, or hotel, you'll hear these immediately. Knowing them removes the guesswork.

In restaurants:

  • 어서 오세요! — eoseo oseyo! — welcome! (said by every server; a nod is fine, no response needed)
  • 몇 분이세요? — myeot buniseyo? — how many people? (hold up fingers)
  • 주문하시겠어요? — jumunhashigeseoyo? — are you ready to order? (wave gently + say jamkkanmanyo if not)
  • 기다려 주세요 — gidaryeo juseyo — please wait a moment
  • 카드요, 현금이요? — kadeunyo, hyeongeumiyo? — card or cash? (point to your card or pocket)
  • 영수증 드릴까요? — yeongsujeung deurilkkayo? — would you like a receipt? Say ne (yes) — keep it for VAT refund
  • 맛있게 드세요! — masikke deuseyo! — enjoy your meal! (nod is fine)

In shops:

  • 어떤 거 찾으세요? — eotteon geo chaeuseyo? — what are you looking for? (open Papago or point)
  • 품절이에요 — pumjeoliheyo — out of stock / sold out
  • 세금 환급 되세요? — segeum hwangeup doeseyo? — would you like a tax refund? Say ne and show your passport to get the VAT refund in-store.

items: 영업 중 (yeongop jung) — open · 영업 종료 (yeongop jongnyo) — closed
할인 (halin) — discount · 현금 전용 (hyeongeum jeonyong) — cash only

sections.title: Key transit routes
steps: Incheon → Melody House (Hongdae): Air Premia arrives at Terminal 1 — the AREX station is inside T1. Take the AREX All-Stop directly to Hongik University Station (≈49 min, ≈₩4,050) — one train, no transfer. The Express costs ≈₩13,000 and skips Hongik University — do not board it. From Hongik University Station Exit 3 or 9: walk (~700 m, ~12 min) or taxi (~5 min, ≈₩5,000–6,000) to Melody House. Total airport → door: ~60 min.

Around Seoul from Melody House (Hongik University Station — Lines 2 + AREX + Gyeongui-Jungang): subway signed in English — tap T-money in and out; use Kakao T for taxis. Key hops from Hongik University Station: Seoul Station (KTX) → Gyeongui-Jungang Line 3 stops (~8 min, ≈₩1,350); Gwangjang Market → Line 2 east to Dongdaemun H&C Park (~20 min), transfer to Line 1 one stop to Jongno-5-ga (Exit 7–8); Yongsan / HYBE / LoL Park → Gyeongui-Jungang to Seoul Station then Line 1 south 1 stop to Yongsan (~15 min total); Myeongdong / Olive Young → Line 2 east to Dongdaemun H&C Park, Line 4 west 4 stops to Myeongdong (~30 min); SPAREX Dongdaemun jimjilbang → Line 2 east ~10 stops to Dongdaemun H&C Park Station, Exit 14 (~22 min).

Seoul → Daejeon (Jul 11, 2 of you): KTX from Seoul Station (not Suseo/SRT), ~50 min — book on the Korail app or korail.com. Take a morning train departing ≈08:30–09:00 KST to arrive Daejeon by ≈09:20–09:50 — you'll be set up and raiding GO Fest at the EXPO Science Park by 10:00, with no transit cutoff before the 17:00 LBF. Third person stays in Seoul for GO Fest Day 1.

Daejeon Station → your hotel / DCC II: a taxi is the easy call — ~20 min, ≈₩11,000 (≈₩5,500 each, split two ways). Your Daejeon I-Hotel (27-5 Expo-ro 123beon-gil) is ~130 m — a 2-minute walk — from the arena, so drop bags and walk in. Cheaper backup: Metro Line 1 to Government Complex Daejeon (정부청사역, ~15 min) then a short taxi (<10 min, ≈₩6,500). Skip the direct bus 707 — it takes over an hour. DCC II: 87 Expo-ro, Yuseong-gu.

Back to Seoul (Jul 13): KTX from Daejeon Station, ~50 min — you stay a second night in Daejeon, so you ride back Monday morning, not Sunday night. Book the morning return in advance anyway (post-Finals trains are busy).

Melody House → Incheon (Jul 15): Air Premia YP131 departs ICN Terminal 1 at 21:30 — leave Melody House by ~17:00: walk or taxi to Hongik University Station (~15 min), then take the AREX All-Stop directly to ICN T1 (≈49 min, ≈₩4,050) — no transfer needed. You arrive ~18:30 for a comfortable 3-hour buffer. Air Premia uses Terminal 1 — the AREX All-Stop terminates there.

sections.title: Getting to/from Incheon Airport — options compared
sections.body:
All options go from/to Hongik University Station, which is on the AREX All-Stop line — making Hongdae one of the most airport-convenient neighbourhoods in Seoul. Source: MileAsia and iVisitKorea; verify fares before travel.

  • AREX All-Stop (subway train) — ICN T1 to Hongik University Station: ≈49 min, ≈₩4,050/person. Direct, no transfer, most reliable for schedule. Trains every 5–15 min from 05:14. Do not board the Express — it skips Hongik University and goes to Seoul Station only. Best option for arrivals and departures when timing matters. Walk from Exit 3 or 9 to Melody House (~12 min) or take a short taxi.
  • Limousine Bus 6002 — ICN → Hongdae / Hongik University area: ≈70–100 min, ≈₩10,000/person. Slower than AREX (traffic-dependent), but comfortable coach seats and stops close to the Hongdae area. Good if you prefer sitting over lugging bags through the subway. Buy ticket at the bus counter in T1 arrivals. Not recommended on departure day — traffic risk makes it hard to time.
  • Taxi (Kakao T) — ICN to Melody House: ≈45–55 min, ≈₩55,000–65,000 total (≈₩18,000–22,000/person split 3 ways). Fastest door-to-door option; no stairs or bag handling. Best for: arrival day (Jul 9) with all luggage after a 15-hr flight, or any time transit feels like too much. Book via Kakao T app in advance — select 'Large Taxi' or 'Kakao T Intercity' for airport runs. The Incheon Bridge toll of ≈₩2,000 is included in the estimate.

Recommendation: AREX for the Jul 15 departure (timing is predictable, ≈₩4,050 vs ≈₩22,000 taxi). Taxi for the Jul 9 arrival (you have all your luggage, it's 06:00, and ≈₩22,000 split 3 ways is worth it). The limousine bus is fine for solo travel but adds time uncertainty for an airport run.

sections.title: Tips & tricks — Seoul and Daejeon
sections.body:
Maps: Use Naver Map or KakaoMap — both show exact exit numbers (e.g. Exit 5) which are essential in Seoul's deep multi-exit stations. Google Maps cannot legally access Korean road data at full resolution — it works for landmarks but gives actively wrong transit routing. Delete it from your muscle memory for this trip.

Subway: tap T-money in and out. If you forget to tap out, the system charges you the maximum fare — tap out every time. Announcements are in Korean, English, Chinese, and Japanese. The colored line number and station name on the platform are always in English. When in doubt, count stops.

Taxis: Kakao T shows the estimated fare and driver details before you get in — always use it at night or in unfamiliar areas. Flagging a taxi is fine in the city. In Daejeon, English is much less common; show the driver the destination in Korean on your phone (type the address in Naver Map and hand them the phone). Kakao T works exactly the same in Daejeon.

Paying for food: in most Korean restaurants you pay at the counter when you leave, not at the table. There's no waiting for a check. Some places have a QR code at the table to scan and pay — it still goes to the counter. Kiosks at chain restaurants are touchscreen-only; tap the English flag at the top-right corner.

Call button: every sit-down restaurant table has a call button to summon a server. Press it when you're ready to order — don't wave or make eye contact trying to flag someone down. Some are physical buttons, some are embedded in a screen.

Convenience stores as infrastructure: GS25 and CU are your ATMs, rest stops, phone chargers (some have USB outlets), umbrellas, and late-night kitchens. You will visit them multiple times per day. The hot-food section (fried chicken pieces, tteokbokki cups, steamed buns) is genuinely good and cheap.

Power and charging: Korea uses Type C/F plugs, 220V. Most modern USB-C chargers handle 110–240V automatically — check your brick. iPhones need a plug adapter; no voltage converter required. Many cafés and PC bangs have power outlets at every seat.

Luggage: if you check out but have hours left in Seoul, most convenience stores and Seoul Station's left-luggage counter (level B1, Platform area) store bags by the hour. Melody House (your Hongdae host) may also allow a late bag drop.

Daejeon specifically: it's a smaller city. English signage exists around DCC II and the EXPO district but disappears quickly. Use Naver Map even for short walks — the street layout around Yuseong-gu is less grid-like than central Seoul. The Dunsan-dong entertainment district (~15 min taxi from I-Hotel) is where the restaurants and bars are on Sunday evening.

items.title: Flight — depart Newark (EWR) at 01:30 EDT
items.pace: Travel day · depart EWR 01:30 EDT · ~15 hrs in the air · land ICN Terminal 1 ~05:30–06:30 KST Jul 9
items.note: The jet lag protocol in the Plan section covers exactly what to do on this flight. Follow it.
items.body:
A night flight across the Pacific — the trip starts in the dark and ends with a Korean sunrise.

  • Depart EWR 01:30 EDT. Air Premia YP132 — ≈14–15 hrs, land ICN Terminal 1 ≈05:30–06:30 KST Thu Jul 9.
  • On the plane: Eye mask + earplugs immediately. No alcohol. Water every hour (~250 ml). Melatonin 0.5–1 mg at ~02:00 EDT. Window shade closed — polar route has near-continuous daylight. Eat the breakfast service near landing.
  • On landing: e-Arrival Card QR at immigration → T-money card at arrivals → AREX All-Stop to Hongik University Station (≈49 min, ≈₩4,050 — no transfer). See 'When you land' checklist for full steps.
items.fit: Compression socks; something easy to sleep in on the plane.
checklist: e-Arrival Card QR saved offline (done Jul 7)
Melatonin packed in carry-on (0.5–1 mg for ~02:00 EDT)
Battery pack and all devices fully charged
Eye mask and earplugs in carry-on
Depart EWR — not JFK

items.title: Arrive Seoul — light, bathhouse, early night
items.pace: Arrival day · ICN T1 → Hongik University Station ≈50 min · outdoor light by 09:00 · jimjilbang 17:00–21:00 · bed by 22:30
items.note: The most important day of the trip for jet lag. Follow the protocol exactly — especially the bedtime.
items.body:
After 15 hours in the air, Seoul delivers exactly what exhausted travellers need: a city that works perfectly at 6 a.m.

  • ~06:00 — Melody House. AREX All-Stop to Hongik University Station (≈49 min, ≈₩4,050) → taxi ≈₩5,000–6,000 (~5 min) to 282-6 Seogyo-dong, Mapo-gu. Early check-in arranged. Shower, change — do not sleep.
  • ~07:00 — outdoor light. Cafes open 07:30 in Hongdae. Get outside for 20 min of morning light — the single strongest clock anchor. Spin Hongdae PokéStops if you want (Road of Legends live — skip raiding today, save passes for tomorrow).
  • 10:00–17:00 — stay awake. Hongdae pedestrian zone is right outside. AC break in a café every 90 min. Goal: awake, upright, daylight. Don't commit to sightseeing.
  • 17:00–21:00 — SPAREX Dongdaemun jimjilbang. Line 2 east ~10 stops to Dongdaemun H&C Park Station (~22 min) → Exit 14, B3F Goodmorning City. Entry ≈₩12,000–15,000 pp. Heated rooms + cooling breaks. ⚠ Verify tattoo policy on Klook listing if relevant.
  • ~21:00 — dinner + bed. Ungteori Meats (118 Eoulmadang-ro, ~15 min walk, AYCE samgyeopsal ≈₩14,800 pp, walk-in, open 11:00–23:00). Melatonin 0.5–1 mg at 21:00. In bed by 22:30 — the most important sleep of the trip.
items.fit: Light, breathable clothes. Flip-flops in your bag for the jimjilbang.

TravelPass switched on (text TRAVEL to 4004)
Do NOT nap over 30 min — jet lag protocol
Outdoor light before 10:00 KST
SPAREX Dongdaemun: arrive ≈17:00, exit by 21:00 (Dongdaemun H&C Park Station, Exit 14)
Melatonin 0.5–1 mg at 21:00 KST
In bed by 22:30 KST

items.title: Palaces & old Seoul
items.pace: Full day · Gyeongbokgung ~2 hr · Bukchon ~1.5 hr · Insadong ~1.5 hr · lots of walking
items.note: Heat and humidity are the real workload — water, shade breaks, an iced-coffee stop. Your body clock is 1–2 days adjusted at this point.
items.body:
Fifteen minutes on the subway and you're six hundred years back — grand palaces, stone lanes, and a neighbourhood that still feels lived-in.

  • Getting there: Line 2 east to Euljiro 3-ga (~14 min, 7 stops) → Line 3 north 2 stops to Gyeongbokgung Station (~22 min total, ≈₩1,650). Taxi: ~25 min, ≈₩13,000–16,000.
  • Morning — Gyeongbokgung. Guard ceremony ≈10:00 and 14:00 (⚠ verify current schedule). Free entry with hanbok rental at gate. Last entry 17:30.
  • Midday — Bukchon Hanok Village. Uphill from the palace — real residents, keep voices low. Spin PokéStops as you wander.
  • Early afternoon — Insadong. Tea houses and craft galleries, ~5 min walk from Bukchon. Iced coffee stop before the evening.
  • ~17:00 — head toward Hongdae or any gym-dense area. Tonight is the Road of Legends finale — one of the best raiding evenings of the trip.
  • 18:00–19:00 — Raid Hour: Origin Forme Dialga & Palkia. Best Master League forms (Roar of Time / Spacial Rend), shiny-eligible (1/20), 2–4 trainers. 4–5 passes. See Road of Legends counter charts.
  • 19:00–20:00 — Primal Raid Hour: Primal Kyogre & Groudon. Groudon = quadruple Water weakness, Master League XL Candy, best Xurkitree counter Sunday. 5+ trainers needed — coordinate ahead. 3–4 passes.

Road of Legends ends 23:59 KST. Dinner in Hongdae after 20:00 — dozens of walk-in options right there.
items.fit: Breathable layers; umbrella doubles for sun and sudden rain.
checklist: Gyeongbokgung open today (closed Tue)
Free entry if you rent a hanbok
Respect Bukchon's quiet hours — it's a residential neighbourhood

19:00–20:00 Primal Raid Hour — Primal Kyogre + Primal Groudon (5+ trainers needed; priority Groudon for XL Candy)
Coordinate Primal group beforehand — find raid partners via Pokémon GO chat or Campfire

items.title: GO Fest Day 1 in Daejeon (all day) → MSI Lower Bracket Final
items.pace: 2 of you · morning KTX ≈08:30 → Daejeon · GO Fest 10:00–16:30 at EXPO Science Park · LBF 17:00 KST · 1 in Seoul for GO Fest independently
items.note: Leaving in the morning instead of afternoon gives you the full 9-hour GO Fest window in Daejeon — all three habitats, no transit cutoff, and you're already 130 m from the arena when the LBF starts.
items.body:
The split day: two of you head south to Daejeon for GO Fest and the arena; one stays in Seoul for a full independent GO Fest day.

🚆 08:00–09:50 — KTX to Daejeon (2 of you). Leave Melody House by ~07:45. Walk to Hongik University Station (~12 min), then Gyeongui-Jungang Line 3 stops to Seoul Station (~8 min, ≈₩1,350) — or taxi directly to Seoul Station (~20 min, ≈₩10,000). Board the ≈08:30–09:00 KST KTX south (50 min, Platform varies — check the Korail app day-of; main Seoul Station, not Suseo/SRT). Arrive Daejeon Station ~09:20–09:50.

Daejeon Station → I-Hotel → EXPO Science Park: grab a Kakao T taxi (~20 min, ≈₩11,000 split two ways = ≈₩5,500 each) to the Daejeon I-Hotel (27-5 Expo-ro 123beon-gil, Yuseong-gu). Check in or drop bags at the desk — it's too early for the room but they'll hold luggage. EXPO Science Park is a 2-minute walk east of the hotel; it has active gyms and PokéStops across the plaza. Cheaper backup: Daejeon Metro Line 1 to Banseok Station (~15 min), short walk — but the taxi is faster and barely costs more split two ways.

🎮 10:00–16:30 — GO Fest Global Day 1, Daejeon. You have all three Saturday habitats without a transit cutoff:

  • Stormfire Peaks 10:00–13:00 — Ice/Electric/Fire: Lugia, Ho-Oh, Articuno, Zapdos, Moltres, Raikou, Entei, Suicune (5★, all shiny); Mega Blaziken, Mega Ampharos, Mega Abomasnow.
  • Astral Tides 13:00–16:00 — Psychic/Ghost/Water: Dialga, Palkia, Giratina (Altered & Origin), Xerneas, Yveltal, lake trio, Solgaleo, Lunala (5★); Mega Alakazam, Mega Gengar, Mega Swampert.
  • Dragonflight Summit 16:00–19:00 — Flying/Rock/Dragon: Kyogre, Groudon, Rayquaza, Reshiram, Zekrom, Kyurem (5★, all shiny); Mega Salamence, Mega Pidgeot, Mega Aerodactyl. Raid this one at EXPO Science Park from 16:00 until 16:30, then walk to the arena.

Top priority: Mega Mewtwo X (Super Mega, Saturday-only, global debut) — it appears in every habitat. Spend 3–5 of your 9 passes here. Put the rest on Stormfire (Lugia/Ho-Oh XL Candy) and Dragonflight (Rayquaza, Groudon, Kyogre) in the afternoon. No need to rush anywhere — you're already at the venue.

16:30 — wrap up, walk to DCC II. Leave EXPO Science Park and walk the 130 m to DCC II. Stop at the merch stand before security — Day 1 queue is shorter if you arrive before 16:45. Bag check: DCC II has no bag check; anything over 45.7 × 57 × 19 cm must go back to the hotel (2-min walk).

🏟 17:00 KST — MSI Lower Bracket Final. Passport on you — tickets are name-tied and mobile-only via the NOL World app (no screenshots, prints, or transfers). Best-of-5 match; budget 3–5 hours. The arena runs aggressive AC — that layer pays off here.

After the match: Sungsimdang (DCC 1F, 107 Expo-ro, ≈08:00–21:00) for fried soboro bread if it's not too late, or Ossi Kalguksu Doryong (~0.2 mi from DCC II, knife-cut noodles) for a post-match dinner. Your I-Hotel room is a 2-minute walk.

🏠 The third person (Seoul): Full GO Fest Day 1 in Seoul — all 9 Raid Passes, roam Hongdae and surroundings across the three habitats. Your Melody House room stays yours all week. After GO Fest ends at 19:00: Saturday in Seoul with a free evening is a gift — the Han River is the natural move. Line 5 to Yeouido Station Exit 2–3, grab fried chicken from a spot near the station, eat on the grass by the water. Or go straight to Hongdae for the full Saturday night energy: buskers, street food stalls, arcades, and the option to walk into Mike's Cabin or Dokkaebi later if the mood is right. Text check-ins during the match; reconnect with the group after the Final.
items.fit: Team colours for the arena; a light layer — DCC II runs fierce AC. Quick-dry shirt for the outdoor EXPO raiding (mid-30s °C).

items.title: GO Fest Day 2 in Daejeon → MSI Grand Final
items.pace: GO Fest 10:00–19:00 Daejeon · Grand Final 15:00 KST · 2nd night in Daejeon (no train to catch)
items.note: Your second Daejeon night removes all the pressure — watch the full Grand Final and still raid Sunday's last habitat.
items.body:
The day the season ends — and with a second Daejeon night, you get to enjoy all of it.

🎮 GO Fest Global Day 2 — Daejeon (10:00–19:00 KST):
You're in the Expo/Yuseong district all day. The EXPO Science Park plazas are the densest PokéStop/gym cluster right next to DCC II — raid from your hotel area and on the walk to breakfast. (Hanbat Arboretum is also active but ~1.5 km away; save it for if you need a change of scenery.)

Sunday habitats — posted LeekDuck lineup:
Earthforged Domain 10:00–13:00 — Ground/Steel/Normal: Regirock, Regice, Registeel, Regieleki, Regidrago, Regigigas, Heatran, Origin Dialga, Origin Palkia, plus Tornadus / Thundurus / Landorus / Enamorus (both formes each) (5★). Mega Metagross, Mega Garchomp, Mega Audino.
Verdant Anomaly 13:00–16:00 — Poison/Bug/Grass: Deoxys (all 4 formes), Genesect (all 5 drives), Necrozma, Stakataka, Blacephalon, the Tapu quartet and the Ultra Beasts. In Korea (Asia-Pacific) the regional UB is Xurkitree — you can only catch it on this continent, so make it a must. Mega Beedrill, Mega Pinsir, Mega Sceptile. Raid here until ~14:15, then walk to the arena.
Twilight Battlefield 16:00–19:00 — Dark/Fairy/Fighting: Latias, Latios, Cresselia, Darkrai, Cobalion, Terrakion, Virizion, Zacian, Zamazenta (5★, all shiny). Mega Tyranitar, Mega Gardevoir, Mega Lucario. Overlaps the Grand Final — raid on breaks with arena signal, then finish the window after the match (you're staying the night).

Top priority: Mega Mewtwo Y (Super Mega, Sunday-only, global debut, shiny) — pure Psychic, weak to Bug/Ghost/Dark; in every habitat. Then Xurkitree (Korea-only UB) in Verdant before the 15:00 match.
Secondary: Therian Landorus/Thundurus & Heatran (Earthforged); Latios & Darkrai (Twilight); Necrozma for Dusk Mane/Dawn Wings fusion.

✅ No more conflict: last year's worry — "GO Fest ends before the match does" — no longer applies. With the second night here, the only thing after the Grand Final is dinner. (Shadow Palkia in Shadow Raids runs through Aug 4 regardless, so nothing time-critical is lost.)

🏟 MSI Grand Final, 15:00 KST. The arena is a 2-minute walk from your I-Hotel room — head over by 14:15. Best-of-5; likely ends 18:00–20:30. NOL World mobile ticket required; passport on you.

Celebrate in Daejeon — no train to catch. For a night out, Dunsan-dong (Seo-gu, ~15–20 min by taxi or Metro Line 1) is Daejeon's dining-and-nightlife district — galbi, chimaek and bars; Sinsuwon Galbi (2F Gukje Bldg, 1007 Dunsan2-dong ⚠ verify hours) is a well-known grill. Closer to the hotel, Cham-i-mat Gamjatang (≈0.2 mi from DCC, pork-bone stew ⚠ hours) is ideal recovery food after a long day. Then back to your Daejeon room.

GO Fest priority: Mega Mewtwo Y first (Sunday Super Mega only), then Xurkitree

items.title: KTX back to Seoul → Namsan, Gwangjang & Dongdaemun
items.pace: Half travel day · morning KTX Daejeon→Seoul ~50 min · Gwangjang lunch · N Seoul Tower at dusk · DDP evening
items.note: A deliberately lighter day to recover from the GO Fest/MSI weekend — ride back relaxed, then take the Seoul afternoon at your own pace.
items.body:
The Daejeon crew rides back — the hard-charging weekend is behind everyone. Today is deliberately light.

  • Morning — KTX to Seoul. Relaxed checkout → booked morning KTX (~50 min) → Seoul Station → Gyeongui-Jungang Line 3 stops to Hongik University Station (~8 min) → walk or taxi to Melody House. Third person is already there. A short rest is completely fine.
  • Lunch — Gwangjang Market (광장시장). Line 2 east to Dongdaemun H&C Park (~20 min) → Line 1 one stop west to Jongno-5-ga (Exit 7–8) (~25 min total). Bindaetteok and mayak gimbap. Cash only at most stalls.
  • Late afternoon — N Seoul Tower. Cable car or Bus 03 from Chungmuro Station up Namsan — best timed for dusk. Don't hike in July heat.
  • Evening — Dongdaemun Design Plaza (DDP). Best after dark when the LED-lit building and rose field are on. Surrounding streets shop until 04:00. Dinner near DDP or Line 2 back for Hongdae street food.

(Changdeokgung is closed Mondays — it's tomorrow's plan.)
items.fit: Light clothes; layers for AC-heavy interiors.
checklist: Morning KTX Daejeon → Seoul (booked)
Drop bags at Melody House — a short rest is fine
Cash for Gwangjang market stalls
Cable car or bus up Namsan — N Seoul Tower at dusk
DDP after dark; Changdeokgung saved for tomorrow

items.title: The Secret Garden, last shopping & a Hongdae send-off
items.pace: Full day · Huwon (Secret Garden) 10:15 English tour ~90 min · afternoon shopping · Hongdae night
items.note: Book the 10:15 English Huwon tour ahead (50 online slots per session — it sells out). This is your last proper night, and the flight home isn't until 21:30 tomorrow, so give Hongdae the send-off.
items.body:
The palace garden you saved for last, a final shopping sweep, then the city's best night out — right outside the front door.

  • Morning — Changdeokgung & Secret Garden. From Melody House: Line 2 east to Euljiro 3-ga (~14 min) → Line 3 north 2 stops to Anguk Station (~22 min total). Arrive 30 min early for the pre-booked 10:15 English Huwon tour (70–90 min guided walk, gentle terrain).
  • Afternoon — last shopping. Insadong is a 5-min walk from Anguk. Myeongdong (Olive Young flagship) ~30 min via Line 3 south. Yongsan i'Park Mall (figures, cards, electronics) ~30 min via Line 1. Keep all receipts for the VAT refund.
  • Evening — Hongdae send-off. AYCE KBBQ at MUSSO (~15 min walk from Melody House, open 24 hr ⚠ verify before going), then the Hongdae youth core: Animate for anime goods, PC bangs, arcades, claw machines, buskers, and late-night chimaek. Stay out late — the flight home is tomorrow evening, no early alarm.
items.fit: Walking shoes for the garden paths; comfortable for a long night in Hongdae.

VAT-refund receipts kept for the airport
Last-minute souvenirs
Norebang tonight after dinner — search 노래방 on Naver Map near Hongik Univ. Station; walk-in is fine before midnight
Start packing tonight — finish before checkout tomorrow

items.title: A full last day — then the evening flight home
items.pace: Departure day · flight YP131 departs ICN 21:30 · near-full day in Seoul first · leave Melody House ~17:00
items.note: Your flight is an evening departure (21:30), so you have almost the whole day — check out, store your bags, and make the most of it.
items.body:
An evening flight is a gift: one more full Seoul day before wheels-up.

  • Morning — check out. Stash luggage with Melody House host, or Seoul Station B1 left-luggage counter (~06:00 onward). Day is yours once bags are sorted.
  • Day — choose your finale. Gangnam/COEX (Starfield Library, SMTOWN, Aquarium); Myeongdong + Cheonggyecheon (final Olive Young run); or Yongsan i'Park Mall (figures, cards, electronics). Do any in-store VAT refund on the way.
  • ~17:00 — leave for the airport. Walk or taxi to Hongik University Station → AREX All-Stop to ICN Terminal 1 (≈49 min, ≈₩4,050 — no transfer) → arrive ≈18:30. Air Premia YP131 departs Terminal 1 at 21:30.
